rule_text: When prices are rising with rising volume and rising open interest, new buyers are entering the market (bullish signal); when prices are falling with falling volume and falling open interest, longs are being forced out and the downtrend will end when sellers liquidate (bearish signal); when prices are rising with falling volume and falling open interest, short sellers are covering and money is leaving the market (caution signal); when prices are falling with rising volume and rising open interest, new short selling is occurring and bearish money is entering the market (bearish signal).

Prices falling with falling volume and falling open interest = longs being forced out

Prices falling with rising volume and rising open interest = new short selling

rule_text: Project tomorrow's high and low prices based on today's open-close relationship and positioning relative to yesterday's close, using DeMark's formula with three conditional cases.
entry_conditions: If today's close is below today's open: Tomorrow's projected high = (H + C + 2×L)/2 - L; Tomorrow's projected low = (H + C + 2×L)/2 - H

If today's close equals today's open: Tomorrow's projected high = (H + L + 2×C)/2 - L; Tomorrow's projected low = (H + L + 2×C)/2 - H
Today's opening price relative to yesterday's closing price biases projections upwards (if open higher) or downwards (if open lower)

Bar is not the open of the day AND volatility of the current bar is 2 times the size of the average volatility of that bar AND close is in the bottom 20% of the bar range → buy

rule_text: Adjust moving average period dynamically based on volatility: use longer periods (30) in low volatility (mean ± 0.25 SD) and shorter periods (5) in high volatility (mean ± 1.75 SD), with linear interpolation between these bounds.
entry_conditions: Calculate standard deviation of price changes
When volatility (price change / standard deviation) falls between 0.25 and 1.75 standard deviations from mean, apply linear formula: n_t = (30-5) × ((volatility - 0.25) / (1.75 - 0.25)) + 5
When volatility is below 0.25 standard deviations, use maximum moving average period of 30
When volatility is above 1.75 standard deviations, use minimum moving average period of 5

rule_text: Scale the number of shares to trade inversely to each stock's volatility, allocating a larger proportion of capital to lower-volatility stocks and a smaller proportion to higher-volatility stocks.
entry_conditions: Calculate annualized standard deviation (volatility) of each stock over the past [THRESHOLD_NEEDED] days
Assign a scale factor of 1.0 to the stock with the lowest volatility
Assign proportionally lower scale factors to stocks with higher volatility relative to the lowest-volatility stock
Calculate the number of shares as: (Total Capital / Stock Price) × Scale Factor
